candidates.stmt:
Karen Rowland is an Abbey ward resident, passionate about achieving the best possible quality of life for everyone in Abbey. She is invigorated by our historic town-centre ward which is also at the epicentre of Reading's rapidly evolving future. However, Karen is determined that this progress does not come at the expense of the most vulnerable in our society, nor does it make Reading a town we cannot recognise in 10 years. She lays a large amount of blame directly at the feet of the current government for the sustained austerity that has negatively affected residents' public services and their quality of life.
Whilst completing her Masters degree in Historic Conservation, Karen has been a very engaged community activist, starting the Baker Street Area Neighbourhood Association(BSANA) in 2012 to resolve issues in her own area. Her natural eagerness to meet and engage with residents has helped to get to the roots of some very challenging issues and she has learned the right buttons to push in order to tackle them. She is a great believer in the power of communities to work with the Council in tackling issues; something that she hopes to encourage if elected. 
Additionally, Karen is a proud trustee of both the Reading Association for the Blind and the Reading Community Learning Centre. Both are organisations that work hard to maximise integration and opportunities for our large disabled and BAME communities. 
Karen is a pragmatist and prefers to keep her politics real, effective and focused. She knows that the Labour party's goals of a fairer life for all can be a reality. Moving to Reading as an EU immigrant in 2012, the way Brexit is managed in the years ahead is also of personal concern to her.  She is eager that residents contact her with their concerns.

Promoted by Chris Gavin on behalf of the Reading and District Labour Party, PO Box 8188, Reading, RG30 9LE

I was born right here in Reading Central, in the Royal Berkshire Hospital, and grew up in Spencers Wood.  Like you, I’m tired of feeling that Britain just doesn’t work any more.

After leaving to study Computer Science at university, I returned during the Covid-19 pandemic to train as a teacher at the University of Reading.  I now live here in central Reading, and proudly work for the local NHS in digital services, having previously worked as a civil servant and consultant.

I see first-hand, through my work in the NHS, the disastrous state the country is in - so I’m standing for Parliament to do something about it.  Fixing our NHS, our economy and our country won’t be easy, but with your help, we can get our town the fair deal we know it deserves.

I was born and raised in Battle Ward and have been part of this community for over 40 years.

With a background in corporate work and experience training professionals in mental health and social care, I bring both practical knowledge, lived experience and genuine compassion to issues that matter in Battle Ward.

I've volunteered locally for many years, including running support groups, working in suicide prevention and helping adults from diverse backgrounds build confidence in English and Maths.  My community activities currently include musical performances with a band, for local care homes, which brings me joy as much as the residents(I hope!)

Now retired, I'm standing for Reform UK to offer common  sense representation - respectfully listening to residents, supporting access to services and working to keep Battle Ward safe, inclusive and well served.

Anthony Spencer is a resident of Caversham ward who moved to Reading in 2020. In his free time, Anthony focuses on keeping fit through various outdoor activities; he also has a keen interest in technology and the environment.

Anthony stands for ensuring residents of Caversham receive the services we should expect: walkable pedestrian paths and pavements that aren't submerged with every rainfall. He is also a strong believer in better bus routing to connect Caversham with all the rest of Reading.

I live in Caversham where I was raised, my father having run a sub-Post Office here in the 1970s and 80s. I want Caversham to be a good place for us to live, with fewer flats being built for commuters and more support for local businesses. 
I am standing to offer voters a better alternative, and if elected my priorities include protecting our green spaces and increasing road quality and safety. Councillors are elected to work for you, and that is how it should be, not the other way around.
I am standing as a candidate for the Social Democratic Party (SDP) as I believe they have the best policies for fixing Britain, through stopping mass immigration, developing our agriculture and industry, and promoting community and family life.

I've lived in Mapledurham Ward for nearly 30 years and brought up my family here. It has meant so much to me to be able to serve the community as Ward Councillor for the last 15 years.

When I stood for re-election in 2016 I pledged to sort out the two big issues for our community: the dilapidated Pavilion and a permanent home within the Ward for The Heights School.

I am delighted therefore that I'm delivering on both these promises.

It is wonderful to receive so much positive feedback and to see so many of you out enjoying the revitalised Mapledurham Playing Fields.

Between now and the Elections, do carry on raising council-related matters and I'll do my best to help. To learn a about some of the work I've been doing, do have a look at my Blog: https://www.IsobelBallsdon.com

I hope you will lend me your vote on Thursday 6th May so that I can continue thereafter to work hard for our community.

I'm standing to be a Councillor for Caversham Heights because I care about the local community and the people who make it.  I've listened to hundreds of voters around the ward and I'll stand up for the issues that matter.  My number one priority is road safety, addressing the problems of excessive speed and lack of lack of adequate crossing points.   I'll continue the work I've begun as a candidate by supporting residents to get their streets cleaned, drains unblocked, and potholes filled, as well as working hard on residents' individual problems.  I've always worked hard, and if elected, I'll work every day to maintain and protect the local environment and to ensure quality services for the people of Caversham Heights.

I am a military veteran, a former police officer, a family man, a Christian and a Reading resident. I have served my country many times in many roles both at home and abroad. Caversham Heights deserves a local councillor who will serve them and not shy away from challenges but confront them head on to get the best outcome for you.

Having lived locally for a decade, I’m standing as a councillor candidate to help shape a positive future. With a career in marketing, property and asset management, I will support residents, strengthen services, and back local businesses—ensuring our community continues to grow, prosper, and offer opportunities for everyone.

Jo is a LibDem activist and council candidate for Thames Ward, where she lives. Jo has a Master’s degree and PhD in electronic engineering and worked in telecoms before retraining in her 40s as a social worker. She has volunteered in the past with Reading CAB as a general advisor, and currently works as an advocate with older people and adults with learning disabilities.

She has lived in Caversham for over 20 years, and believes it is crucial for local voices to be heard when decisions are being made about our area and community. 

Her priorities are to campaign on environmental issues, like preserving green spaces, planting more trees, and introducing better recycling facilities, as well as improving public transport and facilities for pedestrians in the area. She has campaigned for the council to place a new bottle bank in the ward and for bringing forwards the introduction of glass collection from the kerb. Like many others living here, she also worries about levels of traffic, air pollution and parking and is keen to challenge the council to move forwards on these issues.

She particularly wants to ensure that local services and transport are accessible to all and will use her skills and experience to speak up for residents in Thames Ward. She says, “Local residents know what will work for us and what won’t, and the best way for the council to use limited resources is to listen first.”

Jamie was born in Reading in 1974 attending Coley Park Primary School for a number of years from 1978. After a spell away from Reading in the 1980s he returned in 1995 to study Cybernetics and Control Engineering at The University of Reading.

Jamie joined the Green Party in 2010 after helping Reading's first Green Councillor Rob White, to be elected.

In 2012 Jamie was elected as the third Green Party councillor on Reading Borough Council. During his four years on the council he sat on the Traffic Management Sub-Committee, Planning Applications Committee, Cycle Forum, Strategic Environment Planning & Transport Committee as well as others.

Jamie is a keen cyclist having recently completed a ride from the equator just north of Quito in Ecuador all the way to Ushuaia at the southern most tip of Argentina.

Having lived in Coley for over eight years, I’m
proud to call this community home. As a father of
four and a local scaffolding and bricklaying
business owner, I understand the importance of
safety, opportunity, and supporting local
businesses. I’m standing as a councillor to use my
experience to help make Reading a safer, more
secure place for families and businesses alike.

Louise Keane is the candidate for Katesgrove Ward where she has lived since 2003 when she first came to Reading as a student. She finished in second place at the last council elections for Katesgrove Ward and it will be between her and Labour this time. Louise is a Social Worker and loves animals and music and is involved in the local music scene in Reading. If elected Louise would continue to work hard all year round to supporting residents in need, tidying up Katesgrove and improving the Whitley Street area. She is also passionate about improving health and social care in the town.

It’s a real honour to stand as your candidate in Katesgrove — in the town I’m proud to call home.

I’m originally from Hong Kong and came to the UK after the social movement. Today, I live and work in Reading, and I’m deeply grateful to the community that has welcomed my fellow Hongkongers and me so warmly. I hope to give back to the community that has given us so much.

Community has always been at the heart of what I believe in. Whether Katesgrove is a short stop in your renting journey or the place you’ve chosen as your forever home, your voice matters. I want to hear your concerns and work with you to address them.

Since being selected to stand in Katesgrove, I’ve been actively involved alongside local community associations and volunteering groups — from weekend litter-picking to bringing residents’ concerns directly to Council meetings. I’m committed to making Katesgrove an even better place for you.

I understand that many of you have lost trust in politics. That’s why I don’t take the opportunity to stand here lightly. I hope to earn your trust, and together we can work for change that you can see and feel.

With your support, we can make Katesgrove even better.

I have lived in Berkshire for the the last 21 years and moved to Kentwood a year ago and have  two children aged 22 and 12.

I have worked across a number of industries delivering complex projects and will bring that experience to the Council to deliver for the residents of Reading to ensure the Council is held to account to do what is right for it’s residents.

Reading Council needs change to ensure accountability and transparency for its spending.

We need to bring pride and growth back to Reading and if elected will focus my time on making Reading a better place for the residents.

Ali is hard-working wife and mother who has lived in Reading for 17 years. Most of her career has been in the NHS and she now owns a small business in change management and improvement. (www.quality-point.uk).

Ali is involved in a range of voluntary work and her work in mental health saw her win a Pride of Reading Award in 2017.

CV can be found on Linkedin link provided.

Brandon is a resident in Norcot Ward, having moved to
Reading following graduation for his first job as a research physicist. He is an avid chess enthusiast and can be periodically seen at the Chess in a Pub social event, and can also be found chatting at ZeroDegrees monthly at the Science in Reading nights.

Brandon is standing up for safer communities by calling on better enforcement against unregulated e-bikes and speeding near our schools, and is standing up for fairness by holding Reading Borough Council to account on access to GP places.

A strong on crime candidate, sick like the rest of the community of the current councillors inability to properly deal with drug crime & anti social behaviour that has plagued the community.

A student at the University of Reading, a political activist, founder of the Conservative society on campus, talked to many residents in Park Ward who admit their are fed up of the current state or Reading and will attempt to drag it back in line with the rest of Berkshire, safer streets, supporting business and keeping the our communities clean and non graffiti-ed

Roy's politics were shaped growing up as a young carer, whilst living in social housing. If elected as your new councillor, Roy will bring a laser sharp focus on safer streets, robust action on fly-tipping, supporting good local businesses and community organisations.

Roy has a successful career in investment banking and sits on the council of the European Movement UK. He will combine his financial and strategic experience, and his personal experiences growing up, to deliver on the promise of co-operative community values for all Redlands residents.

Ulrike has lived by Prospect Park for nearly 20 years, where she is raising her family. She works as a Project Manager for a local business in Reading and understands the importance of listening and acting on issues and concerns. 
Ulrike volunteers in local youth sports and is passionate about giving children equal opportunities in life.  
If elected she will prioritise issues that matter most to Southcote residents:
continue to report fly tipping and littering, work with the police and the Council to tackle  anti-social behaviour, report pot holes and protect Southcote’s green spaces.

After 25 years here, I’ve seen our community decline, and I won’t just sit back and accept it. I care deeply about where we live and the people who call it home. I’ll fight to restore services, protect our resources, and make our streets safe again. Vote Reform UK and stand with me.

Ben Sims is a technology entrepreneur who previously worked for the United Nations.  He believes that individuals and communities know best what solutions will work for them, and will fight to ensure their voices are heard when the council takes decisions.  In his spare time he enjoys exploring Reading and the surrounding countryside with his wife and young children.  If elected, his priorities are getting a fair deal for Southcote residents and services, building the affordable housing we need, where we need it, and protecting our green spaces and woodlands from further development.

I love Reading’s mix of community engagement and entrepreneurial aspiration.  When my children were small I volunteered with their preschool and our GP surgery and I saw how dedicated people with big ideas can transform services and enrich lives. This inspired me to get involved in community activities such as clean-ups and events. I was first elected as a councillor in 2018 and have had the honour and pleasure of representing Thames Ward since 2022. I hold drop-in and doorstep surgeries all year round to meet residents and hear about what matters to you and have resolved problems for hundreds of individuals as well as tackling issues that affect all of us.

Recent wins include:
- Installing safety throw lines along our rivers
- Getting sunken boats removed from the Thames
- New 'tiger' crossings for Richfield Avenue and Gosbrook Road

Current projects include:
- Refurbishing the Kennet Walk footbridge
- Working with the council and Reading College on road safety on Kings Road
- Refurbishing the outdoor spaces on the Orts Road Estate
- Working with the police to tackle anti-social behaviour

As lead councillor for Leisure and Culture I am delivering significant investment into our town using funding from central government and funding organisations. These projects include the new studio theatre; the new central library; our leisure centres, including the Outstanding-rated Rivermead, right here in Thames Ward; and Reading Museum's upcoming new roman gallery... with more exciting announcements to come!

If re-elected I will not only be able to see those projects though but also to keep on working on the things that matter to us in Thames, like air quality, traffic and parking, and ensuring that new developments retain retail and amenity space and provide affordable homes without detrimental impact on the existing community. For example, I intervened with the developers of Carters Court and got them to change their planned fenced boundary so that homes backing onto the

I want to stand as a candidate because I believe local government should focus on delivering the basics properly and efficiently, and that is increasingly not what many residents in Thames Ward and the wider Reading area are experiencing.
Through both my professional experience and my day-to-day life locally, I see the impact of systems that are under pressure and not always delivering. Whether it is the rising cost of living, housing pressures, transport reliability, or access to services, people are feeling that things are becoming harder while getting less in return.

Working in a safety-critical, public-facing role within the rail industry, I deal with real-world problems every day, from disruption, vulnerable passengers, antisocial behaviour, and the need to make decisions quickly and responsibly. That experience has given me a clear understanding of how important it is for systems to work properly, because when they don’t, it directly affects people’s lives.

In my role as a Union Health and Safety Representative, I represent colleagues, raise concerns, and work constructively with management to improve safety and working conditions. This has reinforced the importance of accountability, transparency, and addressing issues early before they escalate.

My approach to local government is built on three principles: practical delivery, financial responsibility, and long-term thinking. I believe councils must move away from simply reacting to problems and instead take a more strategic, investment-led approach. One that generates sustainable income, reduces long-term costs, and reinvests directly into frontline services.

That means focusing not just on today’s pressures, but on building a council that is financially resilient and capable of delivering consistently for future generations.

Locally, that translates into clear, practical priorities:
• Fixing congestion and improving traffic flow at known problem junctions
• Delivering visible improvements in cleanlines

I have lived in Reading for many years, working in the computer industry, and want to help the town continue to be a great place to live and work whilst ensuring voters’ money is spent wisely.
Over my career of decades I have worked in a variety of different technical roles up and down ‘the Uk’s Silicon valley’ and have observed how things can ‘wander off-track’ if not properly supervised. I am semi-retired now and have enough time and energy to help prevent this from happening. The Thames ward is an attractive place to live in the town (especially where I live near the river) and I want to help keep it that way, as well as the rest of Reading.
The SDP is a sane alternative to the failures of the Conservative, Labour and Lib Dem parties, and the absurdities of Reform and the Greens. The party is culturally traditional and economically left-leaning - you really can have the best of left and right in one party!
If I were elected to the council, I see my objectives as being:
-	Ensuring the council concentrated on the necessary core work that makes everyday life in Reading possible (roads, neighbourhood services, social care etc) and avoiding spendthrift policies and vanity projects 
-	Scrutinising proposed new housing development projects to make sure they prioritise the needs of Reading people
-	Focussing on achieving maximum value for council taxpayers when buying in services and supplies from the private sector
So vote SDP, the party for people who love Reading - but don’t want to overpay for it!

Meri O'Connell has been a familiar face in Tilehurst for many years - she has been a councillor there for 14 years and is a tireless campaigner for her community.  She has an unparalleled record of action and hard work in Tilehurst, from helping residents with vital housing and welfare issues, to fixing lamp-posts and getting the council to commit to refurbish the Meadway Rec.

Meri has lived in Tilehurst for decades.  A former youth worker, she is now a teacher in a local school.

Caroline grew up in Tilehurst and lives locally with her family. She is passionate about supporting her local community, having volunteered for local children’s charities. She wants to ensure there is a strong voice representing Tilehurst.

I an very proud to be bringing up my family in west Reading and with my family roots in West Berkshire, I am proud to be the Labour and Cooperative candidate for Reading West.

I am the founder of an award-winning small business that works with charities, social enterprises and growing businesses.

I have been a Whitley ward councillor since 2010 and am committed to working with residents across Reading West on the issues that matter to local people, whether it is tackling crime, more affordable housing or support for our NHS and social care.

I work closely alongside local small businesses to promote our area and as well as working in a professional capacity as a director of Reading Community Energy Society, a cooperative dedicated to producing renewable energy that benefits our local community.

I am passionate about our amazing home of Reading West.  We deserve an active, accessible MP who will put us first. I promise to always stand up for our community.
Greens are working to avoid climate catastrophe

Pieter De Boiserie and family live in Emmer Green where his children attend the local primary school. He is a keen runner and enjoys walking around the local copse with his kids and dog. Pieter also enjoys cycling, and is an advocate for safer roads and more integrated transport and cycling networks.
